description:
You are a system administrator who has just inherited a tiny piece of legacy C code that lives in:

/home/user/old_code/legacy_hello.c

Your job is simply to prove that this ancient program still builds and runs on the current system.  
Follow these steps (2–3 shell commands in total are enough):

1. Compile the source file into an executable named legacy_hello that sits in the same directory (/home/user/old_code).  
2. Run the newly built executable once and capture its standard output **only** (no stderr) to a log file called:

/home/user/old_code/legacy_hello.log

LOG-FILE FORMAT THAT WILL BE AUTOMATICALLY VERIFIED  
• The file must contain exactly one line.  
• That line must be the program’s output string (“HELLO_FROM_1999”) followed immediately by a single trailing newline character.  
• There must be no extra blank lines or whitespace before or after.

Example of the correct log file (including the final newline):

HELLO_FROM_1999\n   ← (this is the only line; “\n” represents the newline)

Do not add any additional text to the file.

When you are done, the automated checker will:

1. Confirm that /home/user/old_code/legacy_hello exists and is executable.  
2. Read /home/user/old_code/legacy_hello.log to make sure it contains exactly the single line described above.

Perform the task in the terminal just as you normally would; you do not need to show the commands here.  Once the build and log file are in place, you’re finished.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (already present when the task begins):

Directory:
/home/user/old_code

Files inside that directory:

1. /home/user/old_code/legacy_hello.c  
------------------------------------------------
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void)
{
    puts("HELLO_FROM_1999");
    return 0;
}
------------------------------------------------
(File ends with a newline.)

2. There is no executable and no log file at start-up.

Expected final state after the agent completes the task:

1. /home/user/old_code/legacy_hello  
   • Regular file, has execute permission for user.  
   • Running it with “/home/user/old_code/legacy_hello” prints exactly:  
     HELLO_FROM_1999\n (newline at end, no extra whitespace).

2. /home/user/old_code/legacy_hello.log  
------------------------------------------------
HELLO_FROM_1999
------------------------------------------------
(The file contains exactly 17 characters: “H…9” plus the single trailing newline.)

These two artefacts are sufficient for the grader to verify success.
name: task_000000_06f9948b
